title_slide: Python for Data Science — Tutorial 2: Variables, Data Types & Structures

slides.heading: Python Will Give You a Wrong Answer Without Telling You
bullet_points: Summing a string column doesn't error — it concatenates
Types encode intention. Python needs to know what you mean.
slides.speaker_note: Open with the silent bug story to create immediate stakes before any syntax.

slides.heading: The 4 Primitive Types
bullet_points: int — whole numbers (28, 100, -5)
float — decimals (3.14, 0.5, -2.718)
str — text ("data", "42", "True") — note: "42" is not 42
bool — True or False only
slides.speaker_note: Emphasise that '42' and 42 are fundamentally different things to Python.

slides.heading: Check and Convert Types
bullet_points: type(x) tells you what Python thinks it is holding
int(), float(), str() convert between types
Always check before calculating — especially on loaded CSVs
slides.speaker_note: Position type() as the first diagnostic habit to build, not an advanced technique.

slides.heading: Lists: Ordered Sequences
bullet_points: Index from 0, slice with [start:end] — end is exclusive
append() adds to the end
List comprehensions: [s for s in scores if s > 85]
slides.speaker_note: Spend time on comprehensions — they represent a mental shift from loops to transformations.

slides.heading: Dictionaries: Named Records
bullet_points: Key-value pairs — one record per dict
Use .get() for safe access (no KeyError on missing keys)
List of dicts = the natural shape of a dataset row by row
slides.speaker_note: The list-of-dicts pattern is how both JSON and CSV rows map to Python — make this concrete.

slides.heading: Functions: Write Once, Trust Forever
bullet_points: def calculate_average(values): — reusable, testable
Default arguments: is_outlier(value, threshold=2.0)
One function replaces 3 copies of the same notebook block
slides.speaker_note: Connect to the personal story about duplicated cleaning code across notebooks.

slides.heading: What's Next: Tutorial 3 — NumPy
bullet_points: These structures scale to millions of rows with NumPy
Arrays replace lists for numerical work
Everything from Tutorial 2 underpins everything in Tutorial 3
slides.speaker_note: End with a bridge that makes the reader want to continue the series.
